speaker: interviewer
text: --Ziegler, could you please describe how things began to get worse around 1939?

speaker: interviewee
text: My oldest brother was in the Polish army, was drafted, which was compulsory service. He served with the engineers squads. And he just returned in the summer of 1939. He was discharged. And there were strong rumors about upcoming war. We knew that the Nazis invaded, already, Czechoslovakia. They occupied Austria, or let me put it more bluntly-- they united with Austria. It was a case of occupation. And we expected that war was coming. Within a month after returning home, he was recalled for mobilization, for the general mobilization. He was recalled to the army and sent to the German border to build fortifications. The war broke out on the 1st of September, 1939. Day before that, the last day of August, we received news where my brother was. So I and my second-oldest sister went out to visit him. It was about 20 kilometers away, [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: So we left early in the morning. We got there, and he returned the late afternoon from work, all sweated up and dirty. We stayed at a farmer's house in the barn, actually, spread out the hay on the ground. There we slept over.

speaker: interviewee
text: First thing in the morning, about 5 o'clock in the morning, he woke us up. He says, you better go home and go quick. The Germans attacked. That was September 1. So we-- and they were ready in full gear, ready to march off. We left [NON-ENGLISH] Village and made our way back home. On the way, we could hear it overhead-- German airplanes flying. We heard explosions and shooting. Somehow we made it back home. Well, within three days the German armies were in our town, occupied. Immediately, they introduced laws and announcements regarding arms, regarding curfews. And any transgression is punishable by death. And they meant it.

speaker: interviewer
text: What were the curfews?

speaker: interviewee
text: The curfews were 8:00 in the morning till 6:00 in the evening. Anyone found after 6:00 would be shot on the spot, [INAUDIBLE]. There was hunger in town. People still had some food, but the next two, three weeks, food ran out. They started rationing. Also, immediately introduced to identify the Jews, [INAUDIBLE] all the Jewish elders from the city, the former elders of the community, to register every Jew, list. And the Jew was identified by means of wearing a white arm band with a blue Star of David on it. Jews were also restricted from going out of town. Just in town, they can remain. And the rations were starvation rations. I remember like today-- it was Erev Yom Kippur. There was no food in the house. I decided-- a friend of mine, a Polish Boy that I used to play with as a child, he was going with his brother-in-law, which was older person already. Well, when he was in the late 20s or 30, he was-- to me, he was an old man. They are going out to villages to see the neighboring villages, if we can buy some potatoes, whatever. So I say, I'll go with you. Of course, I removed my arm band and went with them. I remember we were crossing a bridge, a town called [NON-ENGLISH], which was about 10 miles away from our town. The Nazis were standing at that bridge. At that bridge, they murdered hundreds of Jews-- Jews that were coming back, returning home when the war broke out, had escaped the front line. Then they were returning. There were individual Poles identifying-- this is a Jew. This is a Jew. They would throw them into the river. And the one who could swim, they would practice shoot, shoot him. And the ones that couldn't swim drowned. I was crossing that bridge. The Nazis there, and I-- they couldn't identify me, really. Even though I was dark, I would be taken for a Gypsy or maybe of Italian descent or something. Number two, I spoke-- like I said before, I spoke fluently Polish, without a trace of an accent. So this is another thing that I could away with it. But my heart was pounding. When we passed the bridge, my friend says, well, Roman, you were scared, weren't you, when you were crossing that bridge, that maybe I will point you out. I says to him, no. You are my friend. You would never denounce me to the Germans. And believe me, my heart was pounding, [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: You didn't believe that.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I told him, you are my friend. And you would never denounce me.

speaker: interviewer
text: I know you said that, but did--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: But did you really believe that, inside?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I did not. That's why I said my heart was pounding--

speaker: interviewer
text: Mm-hmm.

speaker: interviewee
text: --when I was crossing that bridge. He says, yes, you are right. I would not denounce you. You are my friend. I remember we came back. It was already after curfew when we got into the house. I brought home a bag of potatoes, and that was worth every effort.

speaker: interviewer
text: That was, sorry?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was worth every effort.

speaker: interviewer
text: Every effort-- the curfew was only for the Jewish people, or for the Poles as well?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was for Poles as well.

speaker: interviewer
text: But they didn't have to wear-- they didn't have to wear anything to-- it was only the Jew who wore the white arm band.

speaker: interviewee
text: No, Poles were not identified to wear any special identification.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah, but they still couldn't go out--

speaker: interviewee
text: The Jews.

speaker: interviewer
text: --after 6:00.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, they are still on the curfew. Eventually, the curfew was removed for the Poles, about a month and a half or a month later.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mm-hmm, so what happened after this?

speaker: interviewee
text: Afterwards, [? I stood-- ?] they ordered the Jewish community to supply x number of Jews to work in town, like doing manual jobs, like cleaning the roads, digging trenches where it was needed-- for instance, in the winter, snow shoveling, clearing the roads and sidewalks of snow. The Jews had to supply the people for it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who made the decision? Which Jewish people made the decision?

speaker: interviewee
text: The Jewish community-- the Nazi authorities just instructed them, they need 200 or 300 people to supply. And that was the case. So--

speaker: interviewer
text: So who chose-- which of the Jewish people chose which Jewish people went?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was in the Jewish community. They divided, right away, groups. We need 50 for that group. We need 50 for that group, and so forth. So they divided into groups and sent us to various groups. And there usually would be a foreman, a Pole or a German, you know, who made us do the work.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: Or sometimes we worked for Germans, you know, for the Nazis. They moved in, [INAUDIBLE] cleaning their houses and doing various manual works, digging up the gardens and all this.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how were the Jews treated while they were working?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not really pleasantly-- it wasn't uncommon to be kicked or hit with the butt of a gun or with a whip, hit over the head.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mm, and what about food?

speaker: interviewee
text: Food-- you got the food, what you got the rations.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what were the rations?

speaker: interviewee
text: Starvation rations.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah, but what-- what exactly was it?

speaker: interviewee
text: What exactly was-- about 250 grams of bread, and you got about hundred grams of margarine per week, per week. And bread was per day, 250 grams. And you didn't get any sugar. You didn't get any vegetables. Occasionally, you used to get potatoes.

speaker: interviewer
text: So people were selling off whatever they could to Polish farmers. They would-- sometimes they'd come in, the farmers, to buy coal, to town. And Jews would buy from them some potatoes or whatever they could, selling, if they had, jewelry, or selling their clothes, or selling bedding, stuff like that-- trading for food. How long did this situation go on for?

speaker: interviewee
text: This went on almost to 1942. In 1940, they took the first group of Jews to send to Germany, or [NON-ENGLISH], which before the war was Germany. Like, our part where I was live was included in the Germany right away. We became the Third Reich where I lived. We weren't anymore considered as Poland, but Germany.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they sent the first group of Jews to [NON-ENGLISH] to work on the Reich's Autobahn, or the superhighways that the Nazis built. My brother next to me, Jacob, was taken with that group and sent away. In 1941, the youngest sister, one that was next to me, was picked up also and sent away, also to [NON-ENGLISH]-- Bella.

speaker: interviewee
text: --to one of the weaving factories to work there. At the end of 1941, I landed a legitimate job already. I landed a job in the [NON-ENGLISH]. The owner of that [NON-ENGLISH] was a German that lived in Poland before the war. He owned that [NON-ENGLISH]. In fact, my father used to have contracts for him, for the roofs [? through ?] [? the war. ?] I was hired by him to work there. There were about a half a dozen Jews working there that he employed us taking away bottles from the machine for the cooling, which we'd take to a transmission belt, and they are gradually cooled after they are blown. I worked there for a while, and the fellow that worked on the machine was a Polack, a Polish fellow, who I knew well. And I ask him to permit me to work on the machine the last five minutes of the shift, so I could learn how to operate the machine. I was always eager to learn something, various work. So he agreed, and he would take-- he would carry away the bottles while I worked on the machine.

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, after a couple of months working like this, I became good at it. One night when I worked night shift, he did not come into work. He was sick. The foreman stands there-- two people on the machine, working. What do we do? So I volunteer. I says to the foreman, I can operate the machine. I can work on it. I try it every time, you know. He says yes, you're pretty good-- five minutes, let's try it. I start working, and the work runs smoothly. That night, the owner comes in for inspection, checking Mr. Beyer. He walks through, and he stops. He sees somebody with the Star of David on his [? windbreaker, ?] working. So he immediately ask him to stop the machine, goes up to the foreman. He says, since when is a Jew working on that machine? And who is that Jew? He says, oh, that Jew is so and so, Ziegler, he says. So he says, you mean Ziegler, the son of the roofer? He says yes. Oh, how is he doing? He says fine, no problems, excellent. So he walks over to me. And he says, you are Ziegler? You are the son of the roofer? I says, yes, Mr. Beyer. He says, when did you learn on the machine to work? So explain him that every day, you know, for the last couple of months, I used to ask the fellow that worked that machine to let me, the last five minutes, work on the machine. So he say, I hear you are doing very well. I says, no problems. I learned it.

speaker: interviewee
text: He says, OK, tomorrow morning, first thing after the shift, come into the office. You'll get hardworking rations-- the cards for the hardworking rations. I picked up those cards. And as far as I know, I was the only Jew that ever had those cards, because those cards were never issued to a Jew.

speaker: interviewer
text: They were only Germans or Poles that received those cards on the hardworking jobs. To receive the rations for those cards, I had to go to a German store, to a supply store. I walk in. The sign on the Germans store is 'Juden und Hunde verboten,' right? Jews and dogs not allowed-- forbidden to enter. The German looks at me, with the Star of David, that I walked in. And he jumps at me. He says, God damn Jew, couldn't you read the sign? So I do not answer him one word. I just take out the ration cards and put them on the counter. He grabs the ration cards, looks at them, and says, where did you steal those?

speaker: interviewee
text: I still don't answer him. I just take out my ID. And I had a special ID to work at night too, because I worked night shift. And I could walk through the Christian areas, or those that were forbidden-- restricted to Jews.

speaker: interviewer
text: He looks at my ID with the picture and then, oh, they are yours. Now, how in the hell did you get those cards? We don't issue those card to Jews. I said, this you have to ask my boss, Mr. Beyer, for who I work. He says, where do you work? I says, the [NON-ENGLISH] at the [INAUDIBLE]. So he doesn't know what to do with me-- to throw me out or actually to serve me, to give me my rations. And he looks at the cards again, looks at my ID.

speaker: interviewee
text: He says, well, since Mr. Beyer gave you those cards, you must be a excellent worker. In that case, you have my permission to come in. You can come in. But I want you to know that you are the only Jew that crosses the threshold into my business, to my store.

speaker: interviewer
text: So he starts weighing me out the supplies. And he says, what do you do? What kind of work? So I explain him, on the machine, blowing bottles. It's hot, very hot, and you sweat lots.

speaker: interviewee
text: [INAUDIBLE], oh, yeah, that's extremely hard work, he says. You are doing it. That's excellent, he says. He finished everything. He says, well, to show you that I am not a bad guy, because you are such a excellent worker, I give an extra piece of sausage. That's from me personally. And he takes the sausage and cuts me off a chunk and gives me. That's from me personally, because you are a good worker. And from now on, you can come in any time to my store. You don't have to worry about anything. That sign does not apply to you. He becomes my buddy after wanting to kill me when I walked in. And those were occasions, cases like that. I worked there till almost 1942 in August. We had the first action that they have taken and the resettlement, what they called. It was still camouflaged, [? the ?] [? thing. ?] You never knew what was going to happen. They put out an announcement that every Jew got to assemble in front of the Jewish community center. There was like a big clearance place to have their IDs verified.

speaker: interviewer
text: When was this?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was in August 1942. I don't remember exactly the date, but was the beginning of August. By this time, I had already-- my two sisters were taken away because they had no jobs. The other two sisters also resettled somewhere, so we never heard from them again. We later found out that apparently they were shipped to Auschwitz and killed.

speaker: interviewer
text: Which two sisters were these?

speaker: interviewee
text: The two oldest sisters--

speaker: interviewer
text: Mm-hmm.

speaker: interviewee
text: Gitte Leah and Chaya Sara.

speaker: interviewer
text: --and Chaya Sara.

speaker: interviewee
text: In August, we assembled all there with our IDs to have them stamped, that there are lots of false IDs floating around. And anyone caught with the old IDs, not verified by the authorities, will be immediately executed. So of course, everybody attends with their IDs. There, they make a selection-- the old people, children, babies to one side, young people without proper jobs to another side, and those with the proper jobs sent away right away-- discharged to send out, to go back to work where they have to go. Now, I come up with my family together.

speaker: interviewer
text: So the Masha, the other sister, my mother are sent, right away, to one side. And my father, my oldest brother, and myself to go-- to go out, because my father had papers, was worked for a German company, [INAUDIBLE]. And I worked in the [NON-ENGLISH], and my oldest brother worked in the [NON-ENGLISH] then, so we had good papers. So my father ask him, where are they going? They are going to be resettled. They are going to a farm somewheres. So my father says, I am not going without them. So he says, well, then you go with them-- sent my father with my mother and my sister. We never heard from them again. Apparently, they went straight to Auschwitz, were gassed.

speaker: interviewee
text: I was sent home, and I continued to work in the [NON-ENGLISH], my oldest brother. Eventually, I became sick. That was already in the fall, late September. And I did not show up for work for about four days. I developed what you call gastritis, something where I had constant diarrhea. And I could not eat, could not keep down anything. I was picked up in the middle of the night by police, sent to a holding camp in [NON-ENGLISH], which was the neighboring town. And after a week, I was sent away to a forced labor camp, to Brande. That was one of the cruelest and most horrible camps that I experienced in my two and a half years, almost two years and eight months that I spent in the camps, in the various camps, concentration camps. The commandant was a sadistic person. He was a cripple with an artificial leg, from the SA.

speaker: interviewer
text: What's that?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was called the Sturm Abteilung. They were wearing the green uniforms or sometimes black uniforms, they were wearing. They were our guards. He was the commandant of them. He devised means and ways of torturing. They called that camp Erholungslager or convalescent camp, where they were sending in sick prisoners from other forced labor camps, there to recuperate. If it did not get back on his feet within a week to two, either he was killed on the spot or sent to a-- toa a extermination camp, like to Auschwitz or somewhere else, where he gassed. Others that were extremely sick, they were killed there in Brande, right on spot. They were killed by means of a cold shower. Now, I don't know if you can imagine killing a person just by giving him a cold shower. It's very simple. If the person is tied down, he's sick, weak like he is, and you put a steam of cold water on him, his lungs collapse. And he actually suffocates, grasping for breaths-- maximum, five minutes, less, and he's dead. But it's a horrendous death. Those are the means that they was killing the people by. Number two, every Sunday, that was an entertainment for him to make what he called [NON-ENGLISH] or [NON-ENGLISH]. When the made the role call, everybody had to attend with his coat, even though you worked on a construction site with cement and things, which was full of dust. You had to clean it out. You cleaned as much as you could. And you had to stand in front of him, lift up your coat. And he would tap it with his whip. And of course, always a speck of dust came out. You received 25 lashes. So any new prisoner who came into that camp had to go through that, to receive his 25 lashes. At one time when they were lashing a person and he was jumping extremely, couldn't hold on to the stool where he was bent over-- he couldn't hold on. So he went over to the couple that was holding him down. He says, step aside. I'll hold him down without any effort. He pulled out his stiletto and put it to his neck. He says, now jump. If he jumps, he's dead. So that's how he got the lashes without jumping. I received my first 25 lashes without a peep-- I could hardly walk away, of course.

speaker: interviewer
text: How and where did they lash you? What did they lash you with?

speaker: interviewee
text: With a bull whip or a horse whip. It was a whip about three feet long--

speaker: interviewer
text: And what exactly did they--

speaker: interviewee
text: --from leather. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: What exactly would they do? Would they strip you down?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not strip, just bent over a stool and lash you in the back, on your buttocks.

speaker: interviewer
text: On your bare buttocks.

speaker: interviewee
text: Not on the bare, no.

speaker: interviewer
text: No, right through the clothes.

speaker: interviewee
text: With your pants on, but the pants were a thin cotton. So you felt every stroke. You are black and blue after them. Sometimes you had even the skin cut through your clothes. Skin would be cut, and you are bleeding. On another occasion, it was already the end of '42, that one night we worked still at the construction site, dismantling everything. And we used to bring home, back to the barracks, roots-- old roots from trees that were dug up to use, because in the barrack was a cast-iron stove, like wood-burning stove. And we would make fire, because for cover, you had only a thin flannelette blanket to cover yourself.

speaker: interviewer
text: So the barracks were extremely cold. They were not insulated or anything, so we were burning fire. And at that time, we had to have like a guarding the barracks, like night duty. Two prisoners had to be in the barracks, that if a prisoner had to go to the outhouse at night, one of the guards had to go with him, from the prisoners, to make sure that he returns, because one of the prisoners escaped about a month previously. And they didn't find him anymore. So that what they installed-- our night duty. And the other was still at the barracks duty. On that night, the commandant comes for inspection. He comes in. The first he ask, how come there is fire going in the thing? So I report to him. I says, sir, nobody gave us order that we are not supposed to have, and it's extremely cold in the barracks. Since we have the roots from the trees that we dug up at the construction site, so we had fuel, rather than to waste it, we keep warm the barracks. Were you speaking in German?

speaker: interviewee
text: In German, or course.

speaker: interviewer
text: You knew German from school.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I knew German because we lived close to German border.

speaker: interviewer
text: To the border.

speaker: interviewee
text: And it was already three years on the German occupation, so I was already quite fluent in German. Step forward, [INAUDIBLE], they assembled all the night duties from all the barracks. There were six barracks in that camp-- took us all into the shower stalls, into the shower room. Everyone was meted out 40 lashes. And of guys, mostly, I was one of the youngest. I was a kid at the time. I was in-- I was 15 years old. So I said, I am not going to give them the satisfaction of crying out, you know, when they lash me. So I bit my lips till they were bleeding, but I didn't let out a peep out of me. When he finished with the 40 lashes, I remember I could only-- I could not straighten out. So I walked away like animal on all fours. I crawled away from the stool. So the commandant went over and grabbed the lash from the couple that was meting out the lashing, and he hit me right across the head. The lash split my head right up-- I still bear a scar here. Blood was coming down my head, because the heads were cleaned, clipped to the skin, no hair then that. And he says to me, you little [GERMAN], which means a pig, dog. You [GERMAN], you little pig, dog-- when he hit me across the head, and he walked away.

speaker: interviewer
text: Then the couple comes over to me. And I'll use his exact words, what he said to me. 'You little son of a bitch, he says. What are you playing? Trying to play hero? You have more luck than brains that he didn't kill you on the spot, because on many occasions, I have seen where a prisoner wouldn't scream from pain, that he would go [INAUDIBLE] with his stiletto. He would kill him on the spot.

speaker: interviewee
text: And he was blue in the face, because from all the prisoners were screaming, and you were the youngest, and you didn't let out a peep. And I hit you as hard as I could.' He says, remember next time, even if you receive five lashes, scream on top of your lungs, because you may not be as lucky next time.
